description: UE5 timer system and delegate/event system: FTimerHandle, SetTimer, ClearTimer, single/multicast delegates, and dynamic delegates

entries.title: Timers — FTimerManager
entries.description: Timers let you call a function after a delay or on a repeating interval without using Tick. They are managed by the World's TimerManager and are the preferred way to handle delayed/periodic actions.
entries.whatItIs: The FTimerManager system that schedules function calls after a delay or on a repeating interval, tied to the game world's time. Timers are managed per-world and automatically respect game pause, providing a clean alternative to Tick-based countdown logic.
whenToUse: Delaying an action by a set amount of time (respawn after 3 seconds, ability cooldown)
Running periodic logic at a fixed interval (damage-over-time every 0.5s, health regeneration tick)
Replacing manual countdown variables in Tick with a cleaner, event-driven approach
whenNotToUse: When you need per-frame logic that runs every single Tick — just use Tick directly
For precise frame-accurate timing in animation — use AnimNotifies or timeline curves instead
When the timer rate is the same as your Tick rate — the overhead of a timer adds no benefit
For UI animations or widget transitions — use UMG animations or FTimeline in Blueprints
commonMistakes: Not storing the FTimerHandle, making it impossible to cancel or query the timer later
Forgetting that timers pause when the game is paused — if you need a timer that runs during pause, use SetTimerForNextTick or real-time timers
Setting a looping timer without ever clearing it, causing it to fire forever even after the owning logic is no longer relevant
Calling SetTimer with the same FTimerHandle without clearing the old one first — this replaces the old timer silently
Capturing 'this' in a lambda timer without ensuring the object stays alive, leading to crashes when the timer fires after the object is garbage collected
keyPoints: GetWorldTimerManager() or GetWorld()->GetTimerManager(): access the timer manager
SetTimer(Handle, Object, &Method, Rate, bLoop): schedule a function call
SetTimer with lambda: SetTimer(Handle, [this](){ DoThing(); }, Rate, bLoop)
ClearTimer(Handle): cancel a pending timer
IsTimerActive(Handle): check if a timer is still running
GetTimerRemaining(Handle): time left until next fire
GetTimerElapsed(Handle): time since last fire
PauseTimer(Handle) / UnPauseTimer(Handle): suspend/resume without clearing
Timers are paused when the game is paused. Use SetTimerForNextTick() for one-frame delay.
FTimerHandle: store this to reference the timer later. Needed for Clear/Pause/Query.

entries.title: Delegates — Single-Cast
entries.description: Single-cast delegates bind to ONE function. They are type-safe function pointers used for callbacks, strategy pattern, and loose coupling between systems.
entries.whatItIs: A type-safe, single-binding function pointer that can be bound to exactly one function at a time. Single-cast delegates are declared with DECLARE_DELEGATE macros and are used for callbacks, completion handlers, and the strategy pattern where one caller expects one response.
whenToUse: Implementing a callback pattern where exactly one listener handles the result (async task completion, request/response)
Strategy pattern — injecting behavior into a class by binding different functions at runtime
When a function needs to return a value via delegate (DECLARE_DELEGATE_RetVal)
Loose coupling between two systems where only one subscriber is needed
whenNotToUse: When multiple listeners need to respond to the same event — use multicast delegates instead
When Blueprint needs to bind to the delegate — use dynamic delegates instead
For events that are conceptually 'broadcasts' with zero or many subscribers
When you need the delegate to be saved/serialized — use dynamic delegates
commonMistakes: Calling Execute() instead of ExecuteIfBound() when the delegate might not be bound, causing a crash
Forgetting that binding a new function replaces the previous binding — there is no stacking with single-cast
Not calling Unbind() when the bound object is about to be destroyed, risking a dangling pointer (though BindUObject is GC-safe)
Using single-cast when the design actually needs multiple listeners, then refactoring to multicast later
Declaring delegates with too many parameters (up to 9 supported) instead of passing a struct
keyPoints: DECLARE_DELEGATE(): no params. DECLARE_DELEGATE_OneParam(Name, Type1): one param. Up to 9 params.
DECLARE_DELEGATE_RetVal(ReturnType, Name): delegate with return value
BindUObject(Object, &Method): bind to a UObject member function (tracked by GC)
BindLambda([](){ }): bind to a lambda
BindStatic(&StaticFunc): bind to a static/free function
Execute(): call the bound function. Crashes if nothing is bound.
ExecuteIfBound(): safe call — does nothing if not bound.
IsBound(): check if something is bound
Unbind(): remove the binding

entries.title: Multicast Delegates — Events
entries.description: Multicast delegates can have MULTIPLE listeners. They are the event system — one broadcaster, many subscribers. Used extensively for gameplay events.
entries.whatItIs: A delegate that supports multiple simultaneous bindings, allowing one broadcaster to notify many subscribers. Declared with DECLARE_MULTICAST_DELEGATE macros, these form the backbone of UE5's observer/event pattern for gameplay systems like health changes, game state transitions, and input events.
whenToUse: Broadcasting gameplay events that multiple systems care about (health changed, player died, round started)
Observer pattern where one object notifies many listeners without knowing who they are
Decoupling gameplay systems — e.g., a damage system broadcasts, and UI, AI, and audio all subscribe independently
When you need zero-to-many subscribers and the broadcaster should not care how many are listening
whenNotToUse: When only one listener should ever handle the event — use single-cast delegate for clarity
When you need Blueprint binding support — use dynamic multicast delegates instead
When the delegate needs a return value — multicast delegates cannot return values
For high-frequency per-frame events with hundreds of listeners — consider a more efficient polling approach
commonMistakes: Not storing the FDelegateHandle returned by AddUObject, making it impossible to unsubscribe later
Forgetting to call Remove() or RemoveAll() when the subscribing object is destroyed, leaving dangling bindings
Assuming a specific call order for listeners — they fire in addition order but this should not be relied upon for logic
Broadcasting from within a listener callback that modifies the delegate's binding list, which can cause undefined behavior
Using multicast when a return value is needed — multicast Broadcast() is void-only
keyPoints: DECLARE_MULTICAST_DELEGATE(Name): no params. _OneParam, _TwoParams, etc.
AddUObject(Object, &Method): subscribe. Returns FDelegateHandle for later removal.
Remove(DelegateHandle): unsubscribe a specific listener
RemoveAll(Object): remove all bindings from a specific object
Broadcast(): call ALL bound functions. Safe even if nothing is bound.
IsBound(): check if any listeners are bound
Clear(): remove all bindings
Listeners are called in the order they were added (no priority system)

entries.title: Dynamic Delegates — Blueprint-Compatible
entries.description: Dynamic delegates use reflection and can be serialized, making them usable from Blueprints. Required for UPROPERTY(BlueprintAssignable) events and AddDynamic/RemoveDynamic.
entries.whatItIs: A reflection-based delegate type that uses FName-based function lookup, enabling serialization and Blueprint compatibility. Dynamic delegates are the only delegate type that can be exposed to Blueprints via UPROPERTY(BlueprintAssignable) and are used by all built-in UE5 component events like OnComponentBeginOverlap.
whenToUse: Exposing C++ events to Blueprints with UPROPERTY(BlueprintAssignable)
Binding to built-in engine events like OnComponentBeginOverlap, OnComponentHit, OnActorBeginOverlap
When designers need to bind events in the Blueprint EventGraph without C++ involvement
When delegates need to be serialized or saved as part of a UPROPERTY
whenNotToUse: Pure C++ systems where Blueprint access is not needed — regular delegates are faster
Performance-critical code with thousands of delegate calls per frame — FName lookup has overhead
When you need BindLambda or BindStatic — dynamic delegates only support UObject member functions
Internal engine subsystems that never interact with Blueprints
commonMistakes: Forgetting that AddDynamic is a macro that requires the &ClassName::FunctionName syntax — not a regular function call
Not matching the UFUNCTION signature exactly with the delegate parameters, causing silent binding failures
Declaring the bound function without UFUNCTION() — dynamic delegates require reflected functions
Forgetting parameter names in DECLARE_DYNAMIC macros — they are required unlike regular delegates
Calling AddDynamic multiple times without RemoveDynamic, causing duplicate notifications on each Broadcast
keyPoints: DECLARE_DYNAMIC_DELEGATE(Name): single-cast, Blueprint-compatible
DECLARE_DYNAMIC_MULTICAST_DELEGATE(Name): multicast, Blueprint-compatible
UPROPERTY(BlueprintAssignable): expose to Blueprint for binding in EventGraph
AddDynamic(Object, &Method): subscribe (macro — not a regular function call)
RemoveDynamic(Object, &Method): unsubscribe
Broadcast(): fire the event to all Blueprint and C++ listeners
Slower than regular delegates (uses FName-based lookup) — use only when Blueprint binding is needed
Parameter names are required in the DECLARE macro: DECLARE_DYNAMIC_MULTICAST_DELEGATE_OneParam(Name, float, Damage)
OnComponentBeginOverlap, OnComponentHit, etc. are all dynamic multicast delegates
